draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Runners want to see current conditions AND pace metrics simultaneously without scrolling
facade.label: Split screen
facade.acceptImplies: User values parallel scanning of weather + performance data; layout should support 50/50 visual weight or side-by-side cards
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers sequential or layered info; single focus area per screen is more important than simultaneous data access

facade.hypothesis: Runners prefer gesture-based navigation (swipe, flick) over button taps for quick interaction while running
facade.label: Swipe gestures
facade.acceptImplies: Navigation should prioritize swipe and flick interactions; minimize tappable buttons; design for gloved hands and one-handed use
facade.rejectImplies: Standard tap-based buttons and menus are acceptable; gesture complexity may create friction for runners in motion

evidence.hypothesis: Runners want to see current conditions AND pace metrics simultaneously without scrolling
evidence.implication: User values parallel scanning of weather + performance data; layout should support 50/50 visual weight or side-by-side cards

facade.hypothesis: Users associate high-visibility, energetic interfaces with alert states and performance tracking
facade.axisTargeted: warm vs cool + organic vs digital
facade.label: Neon glow
facade.acceptImplies: User leans toward vibrant, digital-forward, stimulating mood—possibly wants app to feel intense and motivating during runs
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers softer, more natural, less aggressive visual language—possibly wants calming presence even during active exercise

facade.hypothesis: A friendly, conversational tone will feel more motivating and less clinical than formal weather reporting
facade.label: Casual running buddy
facade.acceptImplies: User wants personality and warmth in the app—encourages playful copy, emojis maybe, colloquial language, personality-driven notifications
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers clean, minimal communication—stick to facts, remove casual language, make interface more businesslike and data-focused

facade.hypothesis: Users want a dynamic, hopeful visual tone that makes running feel energetic rather than clinical
facade.label: Bright gradient sky
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into vibrant, optimistic color palettes with smooth transitions; weather info feels aspirational not just informational
facade.rejectImplies: Move toward neutral, muted, or cooler tones; prioritize clarity and data hierarchy over emotional warmth

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance info at top vs. scrollable detailed forecast layers
Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
Whether user wants calm, focused atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented vibe
Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather only
Whether user prefers smooth, gradual transitions vs. snappy, immediate feedback

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance info at top vs. scrollable detailed forecast layers
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, focused atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented vibe
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather only
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, gradual transitions vs. snappy, immediate feedback
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Runners need quick access to current conditions without losing their route context; a persistent sidebar keeps weather info always visible while the main view stays on the map/route.
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation structure: sidebar vs. full-screen tabs
facade.label: Sidebar nav
facade.acceptImplies: User values persistent access to multiple info layers (weather, splits, pace) alongside the primary running view. Favor sidebar-based layouts with collapsible panels.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers full-screen focus on one task at a time, or finds sidebars cramped on mobile. Favor tab-based or modal flows instead.

facade.hypothesis: Runners need weather AND pace data visible at a glance without reading labels or calculations—numeric tiles beat prose
facade.label: Glanceable metrics
facade.acceptImplies: User favors quick-scan numeric displays (e.g., large 72°F, 8:45 /mi) over narrative summaries; design should prioritize scannable numbers and icons over explanatory text
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers context-rich, sentence-based updates (e.g., 'Mild and breezy, perfect pace window') over raw data; design should embed numbers in narrative flow rather than isolate them

draft.title: RunWeather
draft.summary: A weather app designed for runners, showing real-time conditions, wind impact, and run-friendly forecasts at a glance.
draft.html:
RunWeather

San Francisco, CA
62 °F
Partly Cloudy
Wind
8 mph
Humidity
65%
Feels Like
59°F
UV Index
4

Run Conditions: Good

Mild temperature and light winds make this an ideal time to run. Watch for occasional clouds.

8 mph

64°F
10 mph
